A lack of systematic, large-scale evaluation paradigms to assess LLMs' ability to generate novel research solutions without external aids.
results: Large-scale Generalizer models (Qwen-235B, GPT-OSS-120B) consistently produced high-quality problem statements with low deficit scores (approx. 2.5).
The GPT-OSS-120B model, when used as the internal critique agent, achieved a 74.05% Success Rate, substantially outperforming other configurations (e.g., Qwen-235B at 43.82%).

While perfect rediscovery of human solutions was rare (15-20% under strict evaluation), the 'Novel & Valid' metric remained consistently high, indicating the generation of sound alternative approaches. A human-verified ELO ranking confirmed GPT-OSS-120B as the top-performing LLM configuration with an ELO of 1119.
solution: Introduces AINSTEIN, a two-phase pipeline where a 'Generalizer' LLM extracts abstract problem statements from research papers, followed by a 'Solver' LLM generating solutions solely from these statements.
Implements iterative internal and external critique loops, simulating scientific peer review, to refine both problem statements and generated solutions.
Utilizes a dataset of 1,214 ICLR 2025 papers, ensuring LLM knowledge cutoffs precede publication to prevent data leakage, and evaluates models using LLM-as-a-judge and human-verified ELO rankings.
paper_summary.summary: The AINSTEIN framework evaluates the capacity of Large Language Models (LLMs) to solve AI research problems using only their parametric knowledge. It demonstrates that leading LLMs, through iterative self-critique, can effectively generalize problems from abstracts and generate novel, valid technical solutions, often proposing alternative approaches rather than simply rediscovering existing ones.

title: AInstein: Assessing the Feasibility of AI-Generated Approaches to Research Problems

abstract: We study the process through which reasoning models trained with reinforcement learning on verifiable rewards (RLVR) can learn to solve new problems. We find that RLVR drives performance in two main ways: (1) by compressing pass@$k$ into pass@1 and (2) via "capability gain" in which models learn to solve new problems that they previously could not solve even at high $k$. We find that while capability gain exists across model scales, learning to solve new problems is primarily driven through self-distillation. We demonstrate these findings across model scales ranging from 0.5B to 72B parameters on >500,000 reasoning problems with prompts and verifiable final answers across math, science, and code domains. We further show that we can significantly improve pass@$k$ rates by leveraging natural language guidance for the model to consider within context while still requiring the model to derive a solution chain from scratch. Based of these insights, we derive $\text{Guide}$ -- a new class of online training algorithms. $\text{Guide}$ adaptively incorporates hints into the model's context on problems for which all rollouts were initially incorrect and adjusts the importance sampling ratio for the "off-policy" trajectories in order to optimize the policy for contexts in which the hints are no longer present. We describe variants of $\text{Guide}$ for GRPO and PPO and empirically show that Guide-GRPO on 7B and 32B parameter models improves generalization over its vanilla counterpart with up to 4$\%$ macro-average improvement across math benchmarks. We include careful ablations to analyze $\text{Guide}$'s components and theoretically analyze Guide's learning efficiency.

keyInsights: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) performance gains are predominantly driven by 'self-distillation,' where models learn to convert problems solvable with multiple attempts (pass@k) into single-attempt solutions (pass@1).
Genuine 'capability gain,' solving problems previously unsolvable even with multiple attempts, contributes a smaller but present portion to RLVR's learning progress across various model scales.

Adaptive, in-context guidance applied selectively when all unguided rollouts fail, combined with importance sampling, accelerates the acquisition of new problem-solving abilities without requiring hints at inference time.
originalProblem: The precise mechanisms driving performance gains in Large Language Models (LLMs) trained with Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) were poorly understood, particularly the balance between refining existing knowledge and acquiring new capabilities.

Accelerating the acquisition of genuinely new problem-solving abilities in LLMs during RLVR training remained a challenge, as existing methods often struggled to efficiently help models overcome problems they could not initially solve.
Developing an effective and scalable method to inject targeted learning signals that improve LLMs' reasoning without requiring constant guidance or relying solely on off-policy data was needed.
results: Empirical analysis confirmed that self-distillation is the dominant driver of RLVR learning across Qwen 2.5 models (0.5B to 72B parameters), with capability gain being a minority contributor.

Guide-GRPO consistently achieved superior performance on mathematical reasoning benchmarks, outperforming standard GRPO, Filter-GRPO, and SFT, with a macro-average Pass@1 of 51.03% and Pass@16 of 70.15%.

Guide-GRPO demonstrated robust scalability, maintaining its performance lead over vanilla GRPO with increased context length (4K to 8K) and model size (7B to 32B), achieving 3.39 percentage points higher macro-average Pass@1 for the 32B model with 8K context.
solution: The paper formally decomposes RLVR's performance gains into 'self-distillation' (improving pass@k to pass@1) and 'capability gain' (solving previously unsolvable problems), enabling a quantitative understanding of learning mechanisms.

A new class of online RL training algorithms, 'Guide' (specifically Guide-GRPO), is introduced, which adaptively injects natural language hints into the model's context only for problems where all initial unguided rollouts fail.

Guide-GRPO uses importance sampling to optimize the policy for contexts where hints are absent, ensuring that learning from guided trajectories translates to improved performance in unguided inference scenarios.
paper_summary.summary: This paper investigates how large language models learn complex reasoning when trained with Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR), formally decomposing performance gains into 'self-distillation' and 'capability gain'. The authors introduce 'Guide-GRPO,' an adaptive online RL algorithm that strategically injects natural language hints when unguided attempts fail, demonstrating superior generalization on mathematical reasoning benchmarks and a consistent performance lead across model scales up to 72B parameters.

title: Adaptive Guidance Accelerates Reinforcement Learning of Reasoning Models

abstract: Scientific discovery plays a pivotal role in advancing human society, and recent progress in large language models (LLMs) suggests their potential to accelerate this process. However, it remains unclear whether LLMs can autonomously generate novel and valid hypotheses in chemistry. In this work, we investigate whether LLMs can discover high-quality chemistry hypotheses given only a research background-comprising a question and/or a survey-without restriction on the domain of the question. We begin with the observation that hypothesis discovery is a seemingly intractable task. To address this, we propose a formal mathematical decomposition grounded in a fundamental assumption: that most chemistry hypotheses can be composed from a research background and a set of inspirations. This decomposition leads to three practical subtasks-retrieving inspirations, composing hypotheses with inspirations, and ranking hypotheses - which together constitute a sufficient set of subtasks for the overall scientific discovery task. We further develop an agentic LLM framework, MOOSE-Chem, that is a direct implementation of this mathematical decomposition. To evaluate this framework, we construct a benchmark of 51 high-impact chemistry papers published and online after January 2024, each manually annotated by PhD chemists with background, inspirations, and hypothesis. The framework is able to rediscover many hypotheses with high similarity to the groundtruth, successfully capturing the core innovations-while ensuring no data contamination since it uses an LLM with knowledge cutoff date prior to 2024. Finally, based on LLM's surprisingly high accuracy on inspiration retrieval, a task with inherently out-of-distribution nature, we propose a bold assumption: that LLMs may already encode latent scientific knowledge associations not yet recognized by humans.

author_info.institution: Singapore University of Technology and Design

organization_info.name: University of Science and Technology of China
Singapore University of Technology and Design

keyInsights: Scientific hypothesis generation can be fundamentally understood as the cohesive association of a research background with one to three previously unrelated pieces of inspiration knowledge.
Large Language Models exhibit an emergent ability to identify latent scientific knowledge associations, suggesting they may already encode connections not yet recognized by humans.
A multi-agent, iterative framework incorporating an "Evolutionary Unit" significantly enhances an LLM's capability to compose high-quality, complex scientific hypotheses from disparate information.
originalProblem: Scientific discovery is a labor-intensive, human-driven process, requiring significant augmentation by AI, but LLMs faced limitations in complex natural sciences.

Prior LLM-driven scientific discovery attempts were often restricted to narrow domains and susceptible to data contamination, leading to rediscovery of known information rather than genuinely novel hypotheses.
The task of generating novel scientific hypotheses directly from a general research background was considered "seemingly intractable" for AI, lacking a structured approach.
results: MOOSE-CHEM achieved over 70% accuracy in retrieving ground truth inspirations within the top 0.8% of 3,000 screened papers, validating the LLM's capacity for identifying latent knowledge associations.

When provided with ground truth inspirations, the framework's generated hypotheses showed high similarity to ground truth, with 28 out of 51 papers receiving a perfect Matched Score of 5 in automatic evaluations and 9 papers achieving MS 5 in expert evaluations.

In a "copilot in-the-wild" setting, MOOSE-CHEM significantly outperformed existing LLM-based discovery frameworks (SciMON, MOOSE, Qi et al. 2024) in generating high-quality hypotheses, with expert validation confirming significant innovations for 21 papers.
solution: A novel mathematical decomposition was introduced to break down the complex P(hypothesis|research background) problem into tractable subtasks: inspiration retrieval, hypothesis composition, and ranking.

The MOOSE-CHEM framework was developed as an agentic LLM system, implementing this decomposition through a multi-step process that includes an Evolutionary Unit for robust hypothesis generation via mutation, refinement, and recombination.

A new, expert-annotated benchmark called TOMATO-Chem was constructed using 51 high-impact chemistry papers published after the LLM's training cutoff, ensuring evaluation of genuine "rediscovery" of unseen knowledge.
paper_summary.summary: MOOSE-CHEM is an agentic Large Language Model framework that mathematically decomposes scientific hypothesis generation into tractable steps, demonstrating LLMs' ability to rediscover novel, Nature/Science-level chemistry hypotheses from unseen literature by identifying latent knowledge associations. This framework achieved high similarity to ground truth hypotheses and outperformed existing baselines on a rigorous, expert-validated benchmark.

title: MOOSE-Chem: Large Language Models for Rediscovering Unseen Chemistry Scientific Hypotheses

abstract: While large language models (LLMs) have demonstrated strong performance on complex reasoning tasks such as competitive programming (CP), existing methods predominantly focus on single-attempt settings, overlooking their capacity for iterative refinement. In this paper, we present RefineRL, a novel approach designed to unleash the self-refinement capabilities of LLMs for CP problem solving. RefineRL introduces two key innovations: (1) Skeptical-Agent, an iterative self-refinement agent equipped with local execution tools to validate generated solutions against public test cases of CP problems. This agent always maintains a skeptical attitude towards its own outputs and thereby enforces rigorous self-refinement even when validation suggests correctness. (2) A reinforcement learning (RL) solution to incentivize LLMs to self-refine with only standard RLVR data (i.e., problems paired with their verifiable answers). Extensive experiments on Qwen3-4B and Qwen3-4B-2507 demonstrate that our method yields substantial gains: after our RL training, these compact 4B models integrated with the Skeptical-Agent not only outperform much larger 32B models but also approach the single-attempt performance of 235B models. These findings suggest that self-refinement holds considerable promise for scaling LLM reasoning, with significant potential for further advancement.

keyInsights: Explicitly training LLMs with reinforcement learning for self-refinement can be a parameter-efficient method to achieve advanced reasoning, allowing smaller models to outperform much larger ones.

A 'skeptical' attitude during inference, where models re-evaluate solutions even after passing public tests, is crucial for addressing the unreliability of sparse test data and uncovering latent bugs.
Dense, squared-incentive reward functions are more effective than sparse binary rewards for stable policy optimization in complex, multi-step reasoning tasks like competitive programming.
originalProblem: Large language models often struggle with complex reasoning tasks in competitive programming when relying on single-attempt solutions.
Existing self-correction methods are limited by sparse public test cases, leading to false positives, and typically lack explicit training to optimize the iterative improvement process.
Scaling LLM performance for such tasks primarily depends on increasing model size, which demands significant computational resources.
results: RefineRL-trained 4B LLMs, when combined with the Skeptical-Agent, surpassed 32B baselines and approached the single-attempt performance of a 235B model on LiveCodeBench v5 and v6.

The Skeptical-Agent consistently outperformed other inference-time scaling strategies such as parallel sampling (Random@16) and existing sequential refinement methods (Reflexion@16), demonstrating the value of skepticism-driven feedback.

RL training led to deeper reasoning within each attempt, evidenced by significantly increased average Chain-of-Thought response length, and ablation studies confirmed the critical role of self-refinement trajectory data and the squared-incentive reward.
solution: A 'Skeptical-Agent' is introduced, an iterative self-refinement mechanism that generates feedback based on both failures and a skeptical attitude towards solutions that pass public test cases, prompting deeper bug detection.

Self-Refinement Reinforcement Learning (RL) is developed to explicitly train LLMs to internalize this iterative refinement, using a novel data synthesis paradigm and a squared-incentive reward function to provide dense feedback.
The approach integrates GRPO-based RL training with self-generated refinement trajectories to teach LLMs to 'doubt and debug' their own code, moving beyond static prompt engineering.
paper_summary.summary: This research from KAUST and Microsoft Research presents RefineRL, a framework that leverages a skeptical multi-step agent and self-refinement reinforcement learning to enhance large language model performance in competitive programming. The approach enables compact 4B models to achieve problem-solving capabilities comparable to or exceeding much larger 32B and even rivaling 235B models by internalizing iterative debugging.

title: RefineRL: Advancing Competitive Programming with Self-Refinement Reinforcement Learning

abstract: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) has markedly enhanced the reasoning abilities of large language models (LLMs). Its success, however, largely depends on strong base models with rich world knowledge, yielding only modest improvements for small-size language models (SLMs). To address this limitation, we investigate Guided GRPO, which injects ground-truth reasoning steps into roll-out trajectories to compensate for SLMs' inherent weaknesses. Through a comprehensive study of various guidance configurations, we find that naively adding guidance delivers limited gains. These insights motivate G$^2$RPO-A, an adaptive algorithm that automatically adjusts guidance strength in response to the model's evolving training dynamics. Experiments on mathematical reasoning and code-generation benchmarks confirm that G$^2$RPO-A substantially outperforms vanilla GRPO. Our code and models are available at this https URL.

keyInsights: Naive, fixed-length guidance, despite increasing early rewards, failed to improve overall performance due to a lack of informative policy gradients (low advantage standard deviation).
Optimal guidance configurations (ratio of guided roll-outs, guidance length) are highly dependent on the specific task (e.g., math vs. code) and the size of the language model.
The ideal guidance length for effective learning is not static and must evolve throughout the training process to match the model's current competence.
originalProblem: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR), specifically GRPO, showed limited effectiveness for small-size language models (SLMs) in complex reasoning tasks.
SLMs struggle to autonomously generate the long, high-quality reasoning chains required to achieve verifiable rewards, leading to sparse advantage signals during training.
Previous solutions like knowledge distillation or multi-stage training often precede RLVR or faced performance degradation when applied to more complex problems.
results: G²RPO-A consistently and significantly outperformed vanilla GRPO across various SLMs (Qwen3-0.6B, 1.7B, 8B) on both mathematical reasoning and code generation benchmarks.

The adaptive guidance length schedules in G²RPO-A achieved the best scores across almost all model-benchmark pairs, validating the benefit of dynamic adjustment over fixed or rule-based decay policies.

Integration with curriculum learning further boosted the performance of both vanilla GRPO and G²RPO-A, and G²RPO-A's strategy of retaining and adaptively guiding hard samples proved superior to methods that filter them out.
solution: G²RPO-A extends GRPO by introducing an adaptive guidance mechanism that dynamically injects ground-truth reasoning steps into a subset of SLM roll-out trajectories.
The algorithm adaptively adjusts the guidance length at each optimization step based on the model's real-time average reward, effectively controlling training sample difficulty.
It integrates curriculum learning to sort training samples by difficulty, ensuring the reward signal accurately reflects genuine model progress and stabilizes the adaptive mechanism.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from The Chinese University of Hong Kong, Shenzhen, and Westlake University developed G²RPO-A, an adaptive guidance mechanism for Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO). This method enhances the reasoning capabilities of small-size language models (SLMs) by dynamically adjusting the injection of ground-truth reasoning steps, consistently outperforming vanilla GRPO and fixed-guidance baselines on mathematical reasoning and code generation benchmarks.

title: G$^2$RPO-A: Guided Group Relative Policy Optimization with Adaptive Guidance

abstract: Effective generation of novel hypotheses is instrumental to scientific progress. So far, researchers have been the main powerhouse behind hypothesis generation by painstaking data analysis and thinking (also known as the Eureka moment). In this paper, we examine the potential of large language models (LLMs) to generate hypotheses. We focus on hypothesis generation based on data (i.e., labeled examples). To enable LLMs to handle arbitrarily long contexts, we generate initial hypotheses from a small number of examples and then update them iteratively to improve the quality of hypotheses. Inspired by multi-armed bandits, we design a reward function to inform the exploitation-exploration tradeoff in the update process. Our algorithm is able to generate hypotheses that enable much better predictive performance than few-shot prompting in classification tasks, improving accuracy by 31.7% on a synthetic dataset and by 13.9%, 3.3% and, 24.9% on three real-world datasets. We also outperform supervised learning by 12.8% and 11.2% on two challenging real-world datasets. Furthermore, we find that the generated hypotheses not only corroborate human-verified theories but also uncover new insights for the tasks.

keyInsights: Large language models can be effectively guided to generate and refine high-quality, data-driven hypotheses from empirical examples.
An iterative, UCB-style reinforcement learning approach enables LLMs to learn from extensive data despite context window limitations by focusing on examples where current hypotheses struggle.

The generated hypotheses are highly generalizable, being interpretable by humans and transferable across different LLMs and out-of-distribution datasets, indicating the capture of abstract underlying patterns.
originalProblem: Scientific hypothesis generation is a critical but often opaque and human-reliant process, bottlenecking the pace of scientific discovery.
Traditional methods depend on human intuition, extensive literature review, and painstaking data analysis, making it difficult to scale.
Large Language Models struggle to process and leverage vast numbers of input examples simultaneously due to their inherent context window limitations.
results: HypoGeniC consistently outperformed zero-shot and few-shot LLM baselines across all tasks, with accuracy improvements up to 31.7% on synthetic data and 24.9% on real-world tweet popularity prediction.

The method matched or surpassed fine-tuned RoBERTa and Llama-2-7B models on tasks like headline and tweet popularity, even with less training data, and demonstrated superior out-of-distribution generalization compared to these supervised baselines.

Qualitative analysis confirmed the recovery of known scientific theories and the discovery of novel, interpretable hypotheses in complex social science datasets, such as features of deceptive reviews and popular headlines/tweets.
solution: The authors propose HypoGeniC (Hypothesis Generation in Context), an iterative algorithm for data-driven hypothesis generation.
Inspired by the Upper Confidence Bound (UCB) algorithm, HypoGeniC iteratively selects and refines hypotheses from a pool based on their performance and exploration needs.
It overcomes LLM context limitations by processing small subsets of examples, adding challenging cases to a "wrong example pool" to prompt the LLM for new hypothesis generation.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers at the University of Chicago and TTIC developed HypoGeniC, an iterative, UCB-inspired algorithm that enables large language models to generate high-quality, interpretable hypotheses directly from labeled data. This approach consistently outperforms traditional LLM baselines and sometimes supervised models in classification tasks, recovering known theories and uncovering novel scientific insights that generalize across models and out-of-distribution datasets.

title: Hypothesis Generation with Large Language Models

abstract:
Using AI to create autonomous researchers has the potential to accelerate
scientific discovery. A prerequisite for this vision is understanding how well
an AI model can identify the underlying structure of a black-box system from
its behavior. In this paper, we explore how well a large language model (LLM)
learns to identify a black-box function from passively observed versus actively
collected data. We investigate the reverse-engineering capabilities of LLMs
across three distinct types of black-box systems, each chosen to represent
different problem domains where future autonomous AI researchers may have
considerable impact: Program, Formal Language, and Math Equation. Through
extensive experiments, we show that LLMs fail to extract information from
observations, reaching a performance plateau that falls short of the ideal of
Bayesian inference. However, we demonstrate that prompting LLMs to not only
observe but also intervene -- actively querying the black-box with specific
inputs to observe the resulting output -- improves performance by allowing LLMs
to test edge cases and refine their beliefs. By providing the intervention data
from one LLM to another, we show that this improvement is partly a result of
engaging in the process of generating effective interventions, paralleling
results in the literature on human learning. Further analysis reveals that
engaging in intervention can help LLMs escape from two common failure modes:
overcomplication, where the LLM falsely assumes prior knowledge about the
black-box, and overlooking, where the LLM fails to incorporate observations.
These insights provide practical guidance for helping LLMs more effectively
reverse-engineer black-box systems, supporting their use in making new
discoveries.

keyInsights: LLMs exhibit suboptimal data utilization when only passively observing systems, leading to performance plateaus compared to optimal Bayesian models.

Active intervention significantly improves LLM reverse-engineering capabilities, with benefits stemming more from the iterative process of generating queries and refining hypotheses than from the objective quality of the collected data.
Common LLM failure modes like 'overcomplication' and 'overlooking' are partially mitigated through active intervention, and the 'Analyze-then-Query' strategy is most effective.
originalProblem: Assessing the fundamental capabilities of Large Language Models (LLMs) in reverse-engineering unknown black-box systems, a core aspect of scientific discovery.
Understanding whether LLMs can effectively make inferences from passively observed data versus actively collected data, and if they can actively refine hypotheses.
Identifying common failure modes of LLMs in inductive reasoning tasks and exploring how active intervention might address these shortcomings.
results: When given only passive observations, LLMs' performance quickly plateaus and falls significantly short of optimal Bayesian inference models across various tasks.
Enabling active intervention substantially improves LLM reverse-engineering performance across program, formal language, and math equation black-box types.

The benefits of active learning are largely model-specific, as intervention data collected by one LLM (GPT-4o) showed limited transferability and benefit when passively observed by another LLM (Llama-3.3-70B-Instruct).
solution: Rigorous experimental design evaluates LLMs on standardized reverse-engineering tasks involving diverse black-box systems (program, formal language, math equation).
Compares LLM performance under passive observation versus active intervention, allowing LLMs to query or test hypotheses against the black box.

Utilizes an intervention-yoked design to disentangle the benefits of data quality from the cognitive process of generating interventions, and analyzes specific failure modes ('overcomplication', 'overlooking').
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Princeton University evaluate state-of-the-art Large Language Models (LLMs) on their ability to reverse-engineer unknown black-box systems, revealing that while LLMs struggle to optimally learn from passive observations, their performance significantly improves when allowed to actively intervene and query the system. This improvement is primarily attributed to the active process of generating interventions and refining internal hypotheses, rather than solely from acquiring higher-quality data.

title: Are Large Language Models Reliable AI Scientists? Assessing  Reverse-Engineering of Black-Box Systems

abstract: The application of Large Language Models (LLMs) in accelerating scientific discovery has garnered increasing attention, with a key focus on constructing research agents endowed with innovative capability, i.e., the ability to autonomously generate novel and significant research ideas. Existing approaches predominantly rely on sophisticated prompt engineering and lack a systematic training paradigm. To address this, we propose DeepInnovator, a training framework designed to trigger the innovative capability of LLMs. Our approach comprises two core components. (1) ``Standing on the shoulders of giants''. We construct an automated data extraction pipeline to extract and organize structured research knowledge from a vast corpus of unlabeled scientific literature. (2) ``Conjectures and refutations''. We introduce a ``Next Idea Prediction'' training paradigm, which models the generation of research ideas as an iterative process of continuously predicting, evaluating, and refining plausible and novel next idea. Both automatic and expert evaluations demonstrate that our DeepInnovator-14B significantly outperforms untrained baselines, achieving win rates of 80.53\%-93.81\%, and attains performance comparable to that of current leading LLMs. This work provides a scalable training pathway toward building research agents with genuine, originative innovative capability, and will open-source the dataset to foster community advancement. Source code and data are available at: this https URL.

keyInsights: Mimicking human scientific reasoning through structured knowledge synthesis and iterative idea refinement can unlock LLMs' innovative capabilities.
Decoupling reward assignment from textual feedback is critical for preventing reward hacking in open-ended creative tasks, leading to more substantive improvements.
Distilling higher-order research signals like insight, trending, and serendipity from scientific literature enables LLMs to generate forward-looking and novel ideas.
originalProblem: Large Language Models (LLMs) traditionally function as knowledge processors or task execution tools, with limited ability to autonomously generate novel research ideas.
Training LLMs for open-ended innovation faces challenges due to the absence of clear training data, predefined objectives, and susceptibility to 'reward hacking' from subjective evaluation.
Existing methods struggle to efficiently leverage vast, unstructured scientific literature to foster the generation of genuinely new research concepts.
results: DeepInnovator-14B achieved performance gains of 1.05% to 8.43% on automated evaluation rubrics and up to 93.81% win rate in detailedness compared to its Qwen-14B-Instruct base model.

It showed competitive performance against leading LLMs like GPT-4o, surpassing it in 'Well-Justified Rationale' and 'No Ethical Issues,' and maintaining over 50% win rate in novelty across out-of-distribution domains.
The multi-step refinement process consistently enhanced the quality of generated research ideas across metrics like novelty, effectiveness, detailedness, and feasibility.
solution: An 'Automated Data Extraction and Synthesis' module constructs a structured knowledge base from scientific papers, extracting ideas, modeling relationships, and distilling cognitive-level research signals.
A 'Next Idea Prediction' training paradigm utilizes Reinforcement Learning (RL) to enable sequential generation and iterative refinement of research ideas.
A process-oriented delta reward signal, quantifying stepwise improvement, is employed with a decoupled reward and comment system to mitigate reward hacking and encourage substantive idea enhancement.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from The University of Hong Kong, City University of Hong Kong, Shanghai Jiao Tong University, and Alibaba Group developed DeepInnovator, a framework that trains large language models to autonomously generate and iteratively refine research ideas. The system demonstrated improvements ranging from 1.05% to 8.43% on automated evaluation rubrics compared to its base model and achieved win rates up to 93.81% in detailedness, while also showing competitive performance against larger LLMs in novelty across diverse fields.

title: DeepInnovator: Triggering the Innovative Capabilities of LLMs

abstract:
There is growing excitement about the potential of Language Models (LMs) to
accelerate scientific discovery. Falsifying hypotheses is key to scientific
progress, as it allows claims to be iteratively refined over time. This process
requires significant researcher effort, reasoning, and ingenuity. Yet current
benchmarks for LMs predominantly assess their ability to generate solutions
rather than challenge them. We advocate for developing benchmarks that evaluate
this inverse capability - creating counterexamples for subtly incorrect
solutions. To demonstrate this approach, we start with the domain of
algorithmic problem solving, where counterexamples can be evaluated
automatically using code execution. Specifically, we introduce REFUTE, a
dynamically updating benchmark that includes recent problems and incorrect
submissions from programming competitions, where human experts successfully
identified counterexamples. Our analysis finds that the best reasoning agents,
even OpenAI o3-mini (high) with code execution feedback, can create
counterexamples for only <9% of incorrect solutions in REFUTE, even though
ratings indicate its ability to solve up to 48% of these problems from scratch.
We hope our work spurs progress in evaluating and enhancing LMs' ability to
falsify incorrect solutions - a capability that is crucial for both
accelerating research and making models self-improve through reliable
reflective reasoning.

organization_info.name: Max Planck Institute for Intelligent Systems

title: Can Language Models Falsify? Evaluating Algorithmic Reasoning with  Counterexample Creation

abstract: How can we use AI to discover a new state of the art for a scientific problem? Prior work in test-time scaling, such as AlphaEvolve, performs search by prompting a frozen LLM. We perform reinforcement learning at test time, so the LLM can continue to train, but now with experience specific to the test problem. This form of continual learning is quite special, because its goal is to produce one great solution rather than many good ones on average, and to solve this very problem rather than generalize to other problems. Therefore, our learning objective and search subroutine are designed to prioritize the most promising solutions. We call this method Test-Time Training to Discover (TTT-Discover). Following prior work, we focus on problems with continuous rewards. We report results for every problem we attempted, across mathematics, GPU kernel engineering, algorithm design, and biology. TTT-Discover sets the new state of the art in almost all of them: (i) Erdős' minimum overlap problem and an autocorrelation inequality; (ii) a GPUMode kernel competition (up to $2\times$ faster than prior art); (iii) past AtCoder algorithm competitions; and (iv) denoising problem in single-cell analysis. Our solutions are reviewed by experts or the organizers. All our results are achieved with an open model, OpenAI gpt-oss-120b, and can be reproduced with our publicly available code, in contrast to previous best results that required closed frontier models. Our test-time training runs are performed using Tinker, an API by Thinking Machines, with a cost of only a few hundred dollars per problem.

keyInsights: Enabling LLMs to continually learn from their own attempts during test time allows them to internalize out-of-distribution ideas, essential for genuine scientific discovery.

Discovery problems benefit from tailored reinforcement learning objectives, specifically an entropic objective that prioritizes finding a single maximum-reward solution over maximizing average performance.

Effective state reuse strategies, like the PUCT-inspired approach, are vital for balancing exploration and exploitation in the iterative discovery process, guiding the model towards promising solution paths.
originalProblem: Large Language Models (LLMs) struggle to discover genuinely new solutions for scientific problems that demand concepts beyond their pre-training data.
Existing test-time search methods, which use frozen LLMs, cannot internalize new ideas or adapt their internal representations to unique problem instances.

Standard reinforcement learning objectives are often ill-suited for scientific discovery, where the goal is to find a single, best solution to a specific, hard problem rather than optimizing for average performance or generalization across many tasks.
results: Improved the upper bound for Erdős’ Minimum Overlap Problem to 0.380876, representing a 16x larger improvement over the prior human state-of-the-art than AlphaEvolve’s.
Generated GPU kernels for TriMul that achieved up to 50% faster runtimes on NVIDIA A100s compared to top human solutions, and secured 1st place in two AtCoder Heuristic Contests.
Achieved state-of-the-art performance in single-cell denoising on the PBMC and Tabula datasets, surpassing existing methods and demonstrating broad applicability.
solution: Introduced Test-Time Training to Discover (TTT-Discover), which continuously updates an LLM's weights during inference on a specific test problem using its own search attempts.
Developed an entropic learning objective that exponentially emphasizes higher-reward actions, coupled with adaptive beta scaling to optimize for finding a single maximum reward solution.
Implemented a PUCT-inspired state reuse mechanism that prioritizes states leading to the highest rewards and incorporates a rank-based prior to guide efficient exploration and exploitation.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Stanford University and NVIDIA introduced Test-Time Training to Discover (TTT-Discover), a method that enables Large Language Models to continuously adapt and learn from a single test problem, leading to new state-of-the-art solutions in scientific domains. This approach yielded improved bounds in mathematics, produced GPU kernels up to 50% faster than human-expert solutions, and achieved top rankings in competitive programming and single-cell analysis benchmarks.

title: Learning to Discover at Test Time

abstract:
Given the remarkable performance of Large Language Models (LLMs), an
important question arises: Can LLMs conduct human-like scientific research and
discover new knowledge, and act as an AI scientist? Scientific discovery is an
iterative process that demands efficient knowledge updating and encoding. It
involves understanding the environment, identifying new hypotheses, and
reasoning about actions; however, no standardized benchmark specifically
designed for scientific discovery exists for LLM agents. In response to these
limitations, we introduce a novel benchmark, \textit{Auto-Bench}, that
encompasses necessary aspects to evaluate LLMs for scientific discovery in both
natural and social sciences. Our benchmark is based on the principles of causal
graph discovery. It challenges models to uncover hidden structures and make
optimal decisions, which includes generating valid justifications. By engaging
interactively with an oracle, the models iteratively refine their understanding
of underlying interactions, the chemistry and social interactions, through
strategic interventions. We evaluate state-of-the-art LLMs, including GPT-4,
Gemini, Qwen, Claude, and Llama, and observe a significant performance drop as
the problem complexity increases, which suggests an important gap between
machine and human intelligence that future development of LLMs need to take
into consideration.
title: Auto-Bench: An Automated Benchmark for Scientific Discovery in LLMs

abstract: Reinforcement Learning (RL) has become a key driver for enhancing the long chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning capabilities of Large Language Models (LLMs). However, prevalent methods like GRPO often fail when task difficulty exceeds the model's capacity, leading to reward sparsity and inefficient training. While prior work attempts to mitigate this using off-policy data, such as mixing RL with Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) or using hints, they often misguide policy updates In this work, we identify a core issue underlying these failures, which we term low training affinity. This condition arises from a large distributional mismatch between external guidance and the model's policy. To diagnose this, we introduce Affinity, the first quantitative metric for monitoring exploration efficiency and training stability. To improve Affinity, we propose HINT: Helping Ineffective rollouts Navigate Towards effectiveness, an adaptive hinting framework. Instead of providing direct answers, HINT supplies heuristic hints that guide the model to discover solutions on its own, preserving its autonomous reasoning capabilities. Extensive experiments on mathematical reasoning tasks show that HINT consistently outperforms existing methods, achieving state-of-the-art results with models of various scales, while also demonstrating significantly more stable learning and greater data this http URL is available on Github.

keyInsights: Low training affinity, characterized by high variance in importance sampling ratios and frequent clipping, is a quantitative indicator of training instability caused by distributional mismatch in RL-trained LLMs.

Heuristic hints, which provide conceptual guidance instead of explicit solutions, foster autonomous reasoning and lead to better generalization by encouraging the model to discover solution paths independently.
Decoupling the rollout and policy prompts is crucial for maintaining training affinity and preventing models from developing a dependency on hints, preserving their intrinsic reasoning capabilities.
originalProblem: Reinforcement Learning (RL) for Large Language Models (LLMs) in complex reasoning tasks often suffers from reward sparsity, where models fail to find correct answers, leading to uninformative feedback and inefficient training.

Existing solutions like mixed-policy or hint-based methods introduce a "distributional mismatch" between external guidance and the model's current policy, causing unstable training, conflicting gradients, and poor generalization.
A lack of reliable diagnostic metrics beyond superficial training rewards makes it difficult to assess exploration efficiency and true training stability in RL for LLMs.
results: HINT achieved state-of-the-art performance, with a Qwen2.5-7B model showing an average gain of 9.0% on in-distribution reasoning benchmarks and 7.4% on out-of-distribution generalization tasks.

The framework significantly improved training dynamics by alleviating "EUR Collapse" and maintaining stable Update Consistency, leading to consistently higher "Affinity" values compared to baseline methods.

HINT demonstrated 18.9% higher sampling efficiency by generating a greater proportion of valid (non-incorrect) samples under equal computational budgets, indicating more productive exploration without compromising output diversity.
solution: HINT introduces a novel "Affinity" metric (combining Effective Update Ratio and Update Consistency) to diagnose and quantify the stability and efficiency of RL policy updates by tracking distributional mismatch.

It implements an adaptive, two-stage rollout: standard training is used when rewards are non-sparse, but on reward sparsity, a pre-defined "heuristic hint" (conceptual guidance, not direct answers) is used to augment the query for resampling trajectories.

The framework explicitly decouples the hint-augmented rollout prompt from the policy prompt to ensure hints only stabilize exploration without creating a systematic dependency during inference or policy optimization.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Fudan University, East China Normal University, and Antgroup developed HINT, an adaptive hinting framework that enhances Large Language Models' (LLMs) complex reasoning abilities by mitigating reward sparsity in Reinforcement Learning (RL) without compromising autonomous problem-solving. It improves reasoning performance by an average of 9.0% on in-distribution tasks for a Qwen2.5-7B model and 7.4% on out-of-distribution generalization.

title: HINT: Helping Ineffective Rollouts Navigate Towards Effectiveness

abstract: Programming-by-Examples (PBE) aims to generate an algorithm from input-output examples. Such systems are practically and theoretically important: from an end-user perspective, they are deployed to millions of people, and from an AI perspective, PBE corresponds to a very general form of few-shot inductive inference. Given the success of Large Language Models (LLMs) in code-generation tasks, we investigate here the extent to which LLMs can be said to have "solved" PBE. We experiment on classic domains such as lists and strings, and an uncommon graphics programming domain not well represented in typical pretraining data. We find that pretrained models are not effective at PBE, but that they can be fine-tuned for much higher performance, provided the test problems are in-distribution. We analyze empirically what causes these models to succeed and fail, and take steps toward understanding how to achieve better out-of-distribution generalization. Collectively these results suggest that LLMs make strong progress toward solving the typical suite of PBE tasks, potentially increasing the flexibility and applicability of PBE systems, while also identifying ways in which LLMs still fall short.

keyInsights: LLMs require specific fine-tuning on synthetic program-example data to become effective PBE solvers; pre-trained models perform poorly in this context.
When appropriately fine-tuned, LLMs can perform sophisticated few-shot inductive inference, demonstrating capability beyond natural language-guided code generation.

The approximate posterior likelihood (`-log q_θ(ρ|X, Y)`) is a strong predictor of problem difficulty, indicating that LLMs actively leverage examples to guide program search rather than just sampling from a prior.
originalProblem: Traditional Programming-by-Examples (PBE) systems rely on domain-specific languages (DSLs), limiting their expressiveness and the scope of automatable tasks.
Existing LLM code generation primarily focuses on natural language prompts, leaving it unclear if they possess strong few-shot inductive reasoning for PBE from input-output examples alone.
PBE requires inferring a hidden algorithm from sparse examples, a different challenge than typical LLM code generation, and prior attempts with LLMs showed mixed or poor results.
results: Fine-tuned LLMs achieve state-of-the-art performance, surpassing symbolic and neurosymbolic baselines on list functions (100% on λ² benchmark), text editing, and LOGO graphics.
The approach enables PBE in general-purpose, Turing-complete languages like Python, allowing synthesis of programs that mix operations and incorporate "world knowledge."
Iterative adaptation improves out-of-distribution performance, raising absolute scores by approximately 10% or more across domains, with a substantial 190% relative increase for LOGO graphics.
solution: Develop a synthetic data generation pipeline where an LLM generates programs and inputs, and an interpreter produces outputs, creating a large, consistent dataset for fine-tuning.
Fine-tune pre-trained LLMs (e.g., DeepSeekCoder) on this synthetic data to learn probabilistic inference, predicting programs given input-output examples.

Introduce an iterative adaptation mechanism, inspired by the wake-sleep algorithm, to enhance out-of-distribution generalization by dynamically expanding the training dataset with newly solved problems.
paper_summary.summary: Cornell University researchers demonstrate that while off-the-shelf Large Language Models struggle with Programming-by-Examples (PBE), fine-tuning them with a synthetic data generation pipeline enables state-of-the-art performance across diverse domains like list functions, text editing, and LOGO graphics, allowing the synthesis of programs in general-purpose languages like Python. An iterative adaptation method further improves generalization to out-of-distribution problems.

title: Is Programming by Example solved by LLMs?

abstract: Large Language Models have recently gained significant attention in scientific discovery for their extensive knowledge and advanced reasoning capabilities. However, they encounter challenges in effectively simulating observational feedback and grounding it with language to propel advancements in physical scientific discovery. Conversely, human scientists undertake scientific discovery by formulating hypotheses, conducting experiments, and revising theories through observational analysis. Inspired by this, we propose to enhance the knowledge-driven, abstract reasoning abilities of LLMs with the computational strength of simulations. We introduce Scientific Generative Agent (SGA), a bilevel optimization framework: LLMs act as knowledgeable and versatile thinkers, proposing scientific hypotheses and reason about discrete components, such as physics equations or molecule structures; meanwhile, simulations function as experimental platforms, providing observational feedback and optimizing via differentiability for continuous parts, such as physical parameters. We conduct extensive experiments to demonstrate our framework's efficacy in constitutive law discovery and molecular design, unveiling novel solutions that differ from conventional human expectations yet remain coherent upon analysis.

keyInsights: The synergistic integration of LLM-driven symbolic search with gradient-based differentiable simulation via bilevel optimization is essential for achieving high-fidelity physical scientific discovery.
A balanced strategy of exploitation (cautious refinement) and exploration (daring new ideas) in LLM hypothesis generation leads to more robust and efficient discovery processes.
LLMs, when grounded by high-fidelity physical simulations, can generate genuinely novel and expert-validated scientific insights that go beyond simple memorization or conventional human intuition.
originalProblem: Large Language Models (LLMs) inherently struggle with simulating observational feedback from physical experiments and performing precise numerical calculations crucial for physical sciences.
Existing automated scientific discovery approaches are often highly specialized and lack a universal methodology applicable across diverse scientific disciplines.
A foundational challenge in AI for science is effectively integrating the abstract reasoning capabilities of LLMs with the grounded computational strength required for physical scientific discovery.
results: SGA achieved significantly lower loss values, often orders of magnitude better, compared to four strong LLM-based baselines across 8 challenging tasks in constitutive law discovery and molecular design.
Ablation studies confirmed the indispensable role of the differentiable simulation component in achieving high-fidelity results, with performance dramatically dropping when it was removed.

The framework successfully discovered a complex, 'imaginary constitutive law,' demonstrating true generative capabilities beyond memorization, and proposed novel, physically sensible constitutive laws and molecular structures validated by domain experts.
solution: The Scientific Generative Agent (SGA) framework implements a novel bilevel optimization strategy, decomposing scientific discovery into discrete and continuous problem components.
Large Language Models (LLMs) conduct the outer-level search, generating discrete hypotheses such as physics equations or molecular structures and proposing initial continuous parameterizations.

Differentiable physical simulations handle the inner-level optimization, precisely finding optimal continuous parameters for the LLM's hypotheses and providing grounded, quantitative observational feedback.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from MIT, CMU, UMass Amherst, and MIT-IBM Watson AI Lab introduced the Scientific Generative Agent (SGA), a bilevel optimization framework that integrates large language models with differentiable physical simulations for scientific discovery. This framework achieved quantitatively superior results in tasks like constitutive law discovery and molecular design, yielding novel and expert-validated solutions.

title: LLM and Simulation as Bilevel Optimizers: A New Paradigm to Advance Physical Scientific Discovery

abstract: Autonomous agents powered by large language models (LLMs) promise to accelerate scientific discovery end-to-end, but rigorously evaluating their capacity for verifiable discovery remains a central challenge. Existing benchmarks face a trade-off: they either heavily rely on LLM-as-judge evaluations of automatically generated research outputs or optimize convenient yet isolated performance metrics that provide coarse proxies for scientific insight. To address this gap, we introduce FIRE-Bench (Full-cycle Insight Rediscovery Evaluation), a benchmark that evaluates agents through the rediscovery of established findings from recent, high-impact machine learning research. Agents are given only a high-level research question extracted from a published, verified study and must autonomously explore ideas, design experiments, implement code, execute their plans, and derive conclusions supported by empirical evidence. We evaluate a range of state-of-the-art agents with frontier LLMs backbones like gpt-5 on FIRE-Bench. Our results show that full-cycle scientific research remains challenging for current agent systems: even the strongest agents achieve limited rediscovery success (<50 F1), exhibit high variance across runs, and display recurring failure modes in experimental design, execution, and evidence-based reasoning. FIRE-Bench provides a rigorous and diagnostic framework for measuring progress toward reliable agent-driven scientific discovery.
title: FIRE-Bench: Evaluating Agents on the Rediscovery of Scientific Insights

abstract: Machine unlearning aims to unlearn specified training data (e.g. sensitive or copyrighted material). A prominent approach is to fine-tune an existing model with an unlearning loss that retains overall utility. The space of suitable unlearning loss functions is vast, making the search for an optimal loss function daunting. Additionally, there might not even exist a universally optimal loss function: differences in the structure and overlap of the forget and retain data can cause a loss to work well in one setting but over-unlearn or under-unlearn in another. Our approach EvoMU tackles these two challenges simultaneously. An evolutionary search procedure automatically finds task-specific losses in the vast space of possible unlearning loss functions. This allows us to find dataset-specific losses that match or outperform existing losses from the literature, without the need for a human-in-the-loop. This work is therefore an instance of automatic scientific discovery, a.k.a. an AI co-scientist. In contrast to previous AI co-scientist works, we do so on a budget: We achieve SotA results using a small 4B parameter model (Qwen3-4B-Thinking), showing the potential of AI co-scientists with limited computational resources. Our experimental evaluation shows that we surpass previous loss-based unlearning formulations on TOFU-5%, TOFU-10%, MUSE and WMDP by synthesizing novel unlearning losses. Our code is available at this https URL.

keyInsights: Automated discovery of unlearning loss functions, even with a comparatively small LLM (4B parameters), can match or surpass the performance of manually designed baselines.
Optimal machine unlearning benefits from task-specific loss functions that adapt to the unique characteristics of different datasets and unlearning challenges, which EvoMU effectively synthesizes.
Allowing the LLM to propose a variable training budget alongside the loss function is crucial for preventing over-unlearning and effectively preserving model utility.
originalProblem: The vast design space for unlearning loss functions makes manual search for optimal solutions inefficient and difficult to generalize across diverse unlearning scenarios.

Existing human-designed unlearning losses are often brittle, sensitive to hyperparameter changes, and lack universality, performing inconsistently depending on the 'forget' and 'retain' data characteristics.
Completely retraining models from scratch to remove specific data influence is computationally expensive, necessitating more efficient post-training unlearning methods.
results: EvoMU achieved the highest overall Model Utility (MU) scores on TOFU-5% (0.65), TOFU-10% (0.58), and WMDP, and competitive performance on MUSE, consistently outperforming human-designed baselines.

Discovered losses for various benchmarks demonstrated distinct architectures, such as reference-anchored asymmetric deltas for TOFU and capped relative-ranking losses for MUSE, tailored to specific unlearning problems.

Models unlearned by EvoMU showed superior resistance to relearning, with forgetting scores remaining nearly flat for approximately 800 steps when fine-tuned on forgotten data, a marked improvement over baselines like SimNPO and NPO.
solution: EvoMU employs an evolutionary search procedure where a small, open-source LLM (Qwen3-4B-Thinking) iteratively proposes and refines executable unlearning loss functions.
Candidate loss functions are fine-tuned on a base LLM using LoRA adapters, then evaluated with a standardized unlearning benchmark to determine a scalar selection score.

The LLM acts as both a proposer, generating initial diverse losses, and a mutator, refining 'parent' losses based on comprehensive performance feedback from previous iterations, including specific training history and evaluation metrics.
paper_summary.summary: EvoMU presents an automated framework that leverages an evolutionary search and a 4B-parameter open LLM to discover context-specific machine unlearning loss functions. The framework consistently generated losses outperforming or matching human-designed baselines on benchmarks like TOFU, MUSE, and WMDP, while also exhibiting enhanced resistance to relearning.

title: EvoMU: Evolutionary Machine Unlearning

abstract: Knowledge editing and machine unlearning are two popular approaches for large language models (LLMs) to stay up-to-date. However, the knowledge updating mechanism of LLMs remains largely unexplored due to insufficient, isolated, and small-scale evaluation. For instance, are LLMs similar to humans in modifying certain knowledge? What differs editing and unlearning as training data increases? This paper proposes KnowledgeSmith, a unified framework to systematically understand the updating mechanism of LLMs. We first cast editing and unlearning as instances of one constrained optimization problem. Then, we propose an automatic dataset generator that provides structured interventions across multiple graph levels and data scales, enabling controlled studies of how different modification strategies propagate through model knowledge. Extensive experiments demonstrate nuanced insights over knowledge propagation, plasticity scaling, consistency, and robustness. For instance, our results show that LLMs do not exhibit similar updating as humans for different levels of knowledge, and there exists consistency-capacity trade-off. We hope our findings can offer suggestions to the design of more reliable and scalable strategies. Code: this https URL
title: KnowledgeSmith: Uncovering Knowledge Updating in LLMs with Model Editing and Unlearning

abstract: While LLMs have shown impressive capabilities in solving math or coding problems, the ability to make scientific discoveries remains a distinct challenge. This paper proposes a "Turing test for an AI scientist" to assess whether an AI agent can conduct scientific research independently, without relying on human-generated knowledge. Drawing inspiration from the historical development of science, we propose seven benchmark tests that evaluate an AI agent's ability to make groundbreaking discoveries in various scientific domains. These tests include inferring the heliocentric model from celestial observations, discovering the laws of motion in a simulated environment, deriving the differential equation governing vibrating strings, inferring Maxwell's equations from electrodynamics simulations, inventing numerical methods for initial value problems, discovering Huffman coding for data compression, and developing efficient sorting algorithms. To ensure the validity of these tests, the AI agent is provided with interactive libraries or datasets specific to each problem, without access to human knowledge that could potentially contain information about the target discoveries. The ultimate goal is to create an AI scientist capable of making novel and impactful scientific discoveries, surpassing the best human experts in their respective fields. These "Turing tests" serve as intermediate milestones, assessing the AI agent's ability to make discoveries that were groundbreaking in their time. If an AI agent can pass the majority of these seven tests, it would indicate significant progress towards building an AI scientist, paving the way for future advancements in autonomous scientific discovery. This paper aims to establish a benchmark for the capabilities of AI in scientific research and to stimulate further research in this exciting field.

keyInsights: Genuine scientific AI requires moving beyond knowledge regurgitation to autonomous discovery based on raw data and experimentation.
The design of faithful interactive environments is crucial to enable discovery without implicitly encoding target principles.
Evaluating success needs flexible criteria, accounting for multiple equivalent expressions of scientific principles and potentially applying Occam's razor.
originalProblem: Current AI systems, particularly LLMs, primarily regurgitate existing human knowledge rather than making genuine scientific discoveries.
Existing AI benchmarks are narrow and fail to capture the exploratory, hypothesis-driven nature of scientific inquiry.
AI systems currently lack the ability to conduct experiments, interact with environments, and exhibit the creative thinking necessary for paradigm shifts in science.
results: Passing these tests would indicate AI systems capable of genuine scientific thinking, not just sophisticated pattern matching or knowledge retrieval.
Such AI could accelerate scientific progress by efficiently exploring hypotheses and analyzing data.
The framework encourages the development of AI systems that learn from raw data and experimentation, defining new research directions.
solution: Proposes a framework of seven "Turing Tests" for AI scientists, inspired by historical scientific breakthroughs.
Each test provides an AI with raw data and interactive simulation libraries (e.g., astronomical observations, physical experiment data, chemical properties).
The AI must independently rediscover fundamental scientific principles (e.g., Heliocentric Model, Newton's Laws, Maxwell's Equations) without prior knowledge of the target discoveries.
paper_summary.summary: The paper proposes seven "Turing Tests" to assess an AI's capability for independent scientific discovery, challenging systems to rediscover fundamental principles like the heliocentric model or Maxwell's equations using only raw data and interactive environments, without prior exposure to human-generated knowledge of these discoveries.

title: "Turing Tests" For An AI Scientist

abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated remarkable performance through supervised fine-tuning or in-context learning using gold labels. However, this paradigm is limited by the availability of gold labels, while in certain scenarios, LLMs may need to perform tasks that are too complex for humans to provide such labels. To tackle this challenge, this study explores whether solely utilizing unlabeled data can elicit strong model capabilities. We propose a new paradigm termed zero-to-strong generalization. We iteratively prompt LLMs to annotate unlabeled data and retain high-quality labels by filtering. Surprisingly, we obverse that this iterative process gradually unlocks LLMs' potential on downstream tasks. Our experiments on extensive classification and reasoning tasks confirm the effectiveness of our proposed framework. Our analysis indicates that this paradigm is effective for both in-context learning and fine-tuning, and for various model sizes.
title: Zero-to-Strong Generalization: Eliciting Strong Capabilities of Large Language Models Iteratively without Gold Labels

abstract: The integration of large language models (LLMs) into automated algorithm design has shown promising potential. A prevalent approach embeds LLMs within search routines to iteratively generate and refine candidate algorithms. However, most existing methods rely on off-the-shelf LLMs trained for general coding tasks,leaving a key question open: Do we need LLMs specifically tailored for algorithm design? If so, how can such LLMs be effectively obtained and how well can they generalize across different algorithm design tasks? In this paper, we take a first step toward answering these questions by exploring fine-tuning of LLMs for algorithm design. We introduce a Diversity-Aware Rank based (DAR) sampling strategy to balance training data diversity and quality, then we leverage direct preference optimization to efficiently align LLM outputs with task objectives. Our experiments, conducted on Llama-3.2-1B-Instruct and Llama- 3.1-8B-Instruct, span three distinct algorithm design tasks. Results suggest that finetuned LLMs can significantly outperform their off-the-shelf counterparts with the smaller Llama-3.2-1B-Instruct and match the larger Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct on the admissible set problem. Moreover, we observe promising generalization: LLMs finetuned on specific algorithm design tasks also improve performance on related tasks with varying settings. These findings highlight the value of task-specific adaptation for LLMs in algorithm design and open new avenues for future research.

keyInsights: Task-specific fine-tuning is critical and effective for enhancing Large Language Model performance in automated algorithm design, moving beyond generic LLMs.
Smaller, fine-tuned LLMs can achieve performance comparable to significantly larger, general-purpose LLMs, offering substantial computational savings and increased accessibility.
The proposed Diversity-Aware Rank-based sampling strategy provides a robust method for constructing effective preference data for DPO in complex generative tasks without clear ground truth.
originalProblem: Existing LLM-driven automated algorithm design (AAD) methods rely on off-the-shelf LLMs, causing substantial computational overhead due to numerous queries.
General-purpose LLMs exhibit limited performance gains across different model choices, suggesting a lack of specialized inductive biases for algorithm design.
Algorithm design tasks lack clear ground-truth labels and benefit from exploring diverse, even suboptimal, algorithms, which makes traditional fine-tuning approaches insufficient.
results: DAR sampling significantly increased the 'delta value' (performance difference in preference pairs), leading to improved algorithm design compared to top-k sampling.
Fine-tuned 1B parameter LLMs consistently generated higher-quality algorithms, often matching or outperforming base 8B parameter LLMs in direct algorithm generation and iterative search frameworks.
Fine-tuned LLMs demonstrated robust generalization, outperforming base models on larger instances of the same problem (CVRP-100) and showing beneficial transfer to related problems (TSP-50).
solution: A two-stage fine-tuning framework using Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) applied to open-source LLMs (Llama-3.2-1B-Instruct and Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct).
A novel Diversity-Aware Rank-based (DAR) sampling strategy to create high-quality preference pairs from an iteratively generated algorithm database, balancing quality and diversity.
Efficient fine-tuning utilizing Low-Rank Adapters (LoRA) to prevent overfitting and enable convergence with smaller, computationally expensive algorithm datasets.
paper_summary.summary: City University of Hong Kong researchers developed a Direct Preference Optimization framework with a Diversity-Aware Rank-based sampling strategy to fine-tune Large Language Models for automated algorithm design. This approach enables smaller, fine-tuned 1B parameter models to achieve performance on par with 8B parameter general-purpose LLMs, significantly improving efficiency and accelerating algorithmic discovery.

title: Fine-tuning Large Language Model for Automated Algorithm Design

abstract:
The rapid advancement of large language models (LLMs) has led to significant breakthroughs in automated mathematical reasoning and scientific discovery. Georgiev, G${ó}$mez-Serrano, Tao, and Wagner [GGSTW+25] demonstrate that AI systems can explore new constructions and improve existing bounds, illustrating the growing potential of LLMs to accelerate mathematical discovery. Similarly, Bubeck et al. [BCE+25] show that GPT-5 can meaningfully contribute to scientific workflows, from proposing hypotheses to generating proofs and analyses. Despite these advances, a rigorous evaluation of these models on canonical, graduate-level mathematical theory remains necessary to understand their baseline reasoning capabilities. In this paper, we present a comprehensive benchmark of four frontier models: GPT-5-Thinking, Gemini-3-Pro, Claude-Sonnet-4.5-Thinking, and Grok-4 against the classic curriculum of Randomized Algorithms by Motwani and Raghavan [MR95].
We tasked each model with generating formal LaTeX proofs for a series of lemmas and exercises spanning the textbook. We find that while the top-tier models (Gemini, and Claude) achieve a high accuracy rate (approx. 66%), demonstrating a robust grasp of probabilistic method and formal logic, other models lag significantly in consistency (approx. 40%). We provide a qualitative analysis of the generated proofs, highlighting differences in conciseness, hallucination rates, and logical structure. Our results suggest that while frontier models have reached a threshold of proficiency suitable for graduate-level pedagogical assistance and formalization, significant variance exists in their reliability for rigorous mathematical derivation. The code and the full set of LLM-generated responses are open-sourced and publicly available at this https URL.

keyInsights: Top-tier LLMs demonstrate a notable proficiency in graduate-level mathematical reasoning, indicating their potential for pedagogical assistance and formalization tasks.
Significant variability in reliability and performance exists among frontier LLMs, highlighting that while some can tackle complex proofs, others struggle with consistency and correctness.
LLMs exhibit distinct proof styles, ranging from natural language explanations to highly formal symbolic manipulation or structured textbook-like outputs.
originalProblem: Existing LLM benchmarks for mathematical reasoning often focused on short, isolated problems, lacking deep, multi-step integrative reasoning from foundational theory.
There was no systematic evaluation for LLMs on canonical, graduate-level mathematical theory that demands sustained reasoning across multiple conceptual layers.
The true mastery of LLMs over modern theoretical mathematical reasoning frameworks remained unassessed.
results: Claude-Sonnet-4.5-Thinking achieved the highest accuracy at 66.4%, followed closely by Gemini-3-Pro at 62.1% across the 253 PhD-level mathematical problems.
GPT-5-Thinking scored 47.4% accuracy, while Grok-4 had the lowest at 33.2% and a substantial 52.5% failure rate in producing a proof within given constraints.
Top-performing models like Claude and Gemini exhibited low rates of incorrect solutions (1.9% and 16.6% respectively), showcasing a strong grasp of probabilistic methods and formal logic.
solution: A six-stage benchmark pipeline was developed, using 253 problems from the 'Randomized Algorithms' textbook, involving human-in-the-loop question collection and formalization.
Four frontier LLMs (Claude-Sonnet-4.5-Thinking, Gemini-3-Pro, GPT-5-Thinking, Grok-4) were tasked with generating full, formal LaTeX proofs from the verified problem statements.
An AI model (Claude-Sonnet-4.5) performed automated proof verification, with 20% of its verdicts meta-verified by humans to ensure reliability and consistency.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers benchmarked advanced Large Language Models (LLMs) on PhD-level mathematical reasoning, evaluating their ability to generate formal proofs for 253 problems from a theoretical computer science textbook. They found that models like Claude-Sonnet-4.5-Thinking and Gemini-3-Pro achieved approximately 66% accuracy in multi-step derivations, establishing a new baseline for LLM proficiency in complex academic mathematics.

title: Evaluating Frontier LLMs on PhD-Level Mathematical Reasoning: A Benchmark on a Textbook in Theoretical Computer Science about Randomized Algorithms

abstract: Bold claims about AI's role in science-from "AGI will cure all diseases" to promises of radically accelerated discovery-raise a central epistemic question: do large language models (LLMs) truly generate new knowledge, or do they merely remix memorized fragments? We propose unlearning-as-ablation as a falsifiable probe of constructive scientific discovery. The idea is to systematically remove a target result together with its forget-closure (supporting lemmas, paraphrases, and multi-hop entailments) and then evaluate whether the model can re-derive the result from only permitted axioms and tools. Success would indicate generative capability beyond recall; failure would expose current limits. Unlike prevailing motivations for unlearning-privacy, copyright, or safety-our framing repositions it as an epistemic probe for AI-for-Science. We outline a minimal pilot in mathematics and algorithms to illustrate feasibility, and sketch how the same approach could later be extended to domains such as physics or chemistry. This is a position paper: our contribution is conceptual and methodological, not empirical. We aim to stimulate discussion on how principled ablation tests could help distinguish models that reconstruct knowledge from those that merely retrieve it, and how such probes might guide the next generation of AI-for-Science benchmarks.
title: Unlearning as Ablation: Toward a Falsifiable Benchmark for Generative Scientific Discovery

abstract: Large language models (LLMs) demonstrate impressive capabilities across a wide range of tasks, yet it remains unclear whether such success reflects genuine reasoning or sophisticated recall. We introduce AInstein, a framework for testing whether LLMs can generate valid solutions to AI research problems using only their pretrained parametric knowledge -- without domain-specific fine-tuning, retrieval augmentation, or other external aids. Our approach extracts distilled problem statements from high-quality ICLR 2025 submissions, then tasks specialized solver agents with proposing and refining technical solutions through iterative critique loops, mimicking the cycles of proposal, review, and revision central to scientific inquiry. We evaluate AInstein on 1,214 ICLR papers stratified by acceptance tier (Oral, Spotlight, Poster), using an LLM-as-a-judge paradigm guided by a structured rubric, complemented by targeted manual checks. Performance is assessed with three metrics: Success Rate (does the solution address the problem?), Rediscovery (does it align with human-proposed methods?), and Novelty (does it yield valid, original approaches?). Our results reveal that while LLMs can rediscover feasible solutions and occasionally propose creative alternatives, their problem-solving ability remains fragile and highly sensitive to framing. These findings provide the first large-scale evidence on the extent to which LLMs can act as autonomous scientific problem-solvers, highlighting both their latent potential and their current limitations.
title: AInstein: Assessing the Feasibility of AI-Generated Approaches to Research Problems

abstract: How can we use AI to discover a new state of the art for a scientific problem? Prior work in test-time scaling, such as AlphaEvolve, performs search by prompting a frozen LLM. We perform reinforcement learning at test time, so the LLM can continue to train, but now with experience specific to the test problem. This form of continual learning is quite special, because its goal is to produce one great solution rather than many good ones on average, and to solve this very problem rather than generalize to other problems. Therefore, our learning objective and search subroutine are designed to prioritize the most promising solutions. We call this method Test-Time Training to Discover (TTT-Discover). Following prior work, we focus on problems with continuous rewards. We report results for every problem we attempted, across mathematics, GPU kernel engineering, algorithm design, and biology. TTT-Discover sets the new state of the art in almost all of them: (i) Erdős' minimum overlap problem and an autocorrelation inequality; (ii) a GPUMode kernel competition (up to $2\times$ faster than prior art); (iii) past AtCoder algorithm competitions; and (iv) denoising problem in single-cell analysis. Our solutions are reviewed by experts or the organizers. All our results are achieved with an open model, OpenAI gpt-oss-120b, and can be reproduced with our publicly available code, in contrast to previous best results that required closed frontier models. Our test-time training runs are performed using Tinker, an API by Thinking Machines, with a cost of only a few hundred dollars per problem.
title: Learning to Discover at Test Time

abstract: Reinforcement learning (RL) has become a key technique for enhancing the reasoning abilities of large language models (LLMs), with policy-gradient algorithms dominating the post-training stage because of their efficiency and effectiveness. However, most existing benchmarks evaluate large-language-model reasoning under idealized settings, overlooking performance in realistic, non-ideal scenarios. We identify three representative non-ideal scenarios with practical relevance: summary inference, fine-grained noise suppression, and contextual filtering. We introduce a new research direction guided by brain-science findings that human reasoning remains reliable under imperfect inputs. We formally define and evaluate these challenging scenarios. We fine-tune three LLMs and a state-of-the-art large vision-language model (LVLM) using RL with a representative policy-gradient algorithm and then test their performance on eight public datasets.
